name: Zorya
description: Zorya are the two (or sometimes three) dawn goddesses in Slavic mythology, often depicted as sisters. They are the keepers of the celestial gates, opening them each morning for the sun god Dažbog (or Svarog) to ride across the sky and closing them each evening when he returns. Zorya Utrennyaya, the Morning Star, opens the gates at dawn, allowing Dažbog to begin his journey. Zorya Vechernyaya, the Evening Star, closes the gates at dusk, welcoming Dažbog back for the night. In some traditions, a third Zorya, Zorya Polunochnaya (Midnight Star) is mentioned, guarding Dažbog during the darkest hours. The Zorya are associated with light, protection, and the cycle of day and night. They are often invoked for protection, especially for warriors. They are also seen as symbols of hope and new beginnings. Their role is crucial in maintaining the cosmic order, ensuring the sun's daily journey and the continuation of life. They are also linked to horses, especially the horses of Dažbog, and are said to rest near the constellation Ursa Minor. The Zorya's dedication to their duty ensures the continuation of life and light in the world, making them vital figures in Slavic beliefs. Some interpretations suggest connections to weaving and fate, further emphasizing their role in the cosmic tapestry.
subtitle: Dawn Goddesses, Keepers of the Gates

keyMyths.title: The Opening of the Gates
keyMyths.description: Zorya Utrennyaya opens the celestial gates each morning, allowing Dažbog to ride his chariot across the sky, bringing light to the world.

keyMyths.title: The Closing of the Gates
keyMyths.description: Zorya Vechernyaya closes the gates at dusk, welcoming Dažbog back after his journey and ensuring the safety of the night.

keyMyths.title: Protection of Warriors
keyMyths.description: Warriors would pray to the Zorya for protection before battle, believing the goddesses' light would guide and shield them from harm.

sources.author: Gieysztor, Aleksander
